nodes.statement: A MedBrief super-admin sees three matter-creation options: add a new client, create a matter (classic), and create a matter (wizard). Regular clients see only the classic or wizard options.

matter_creation flow diagram; Columns A B C D E F G H; services; matter detail; patient detail; records request; early case assessment; sort & paginate; chronology; records collection; supporting documents; summary

nodes.statement: The matter creation wizard always shows default pages (services, matter detail, patient detail, supporting documents, summary). Additional service-specific pages (records request, early case assessment, sort and paginate, chronology, records collection) are shown conditionally based on the services selected on the first page.

nodes.label: Supporting documents page collects consent forms and death certificates for records requests

nodes.statement: The supporting documents page collects consent forms (form of authority) required for records requests and, if the patient is deceased, a death certificate.

nodes.statement: Service defaults can be set at the client level so that services are pre-selected when a new matter is created. This applies mainly to the services page of the wizard.

nodes.label: If no additional services are selected, the client pays only for hosting

nodes.statement: When a client selects no additional services, they are charged only for hosting of the records.

nodes.label: MedBrief is venturing into personal injury, but proportion of PI matters is still small

nodes.statement: MedBrief is expanding into personal injury matters but this remains a small proportion of overall volume compared to clinical negligence.

nodes.statement: The terms "area" and "client" are used interchangeably but inconsistently across the MedBrief product. Wherever "area" appears, it refers to the client.

nodes.label: Clinical negligence has the full set of services; personal injury has fewer

provenance.quote: personal injury has less services um whereas clinical negligence has has has the full monty the the services that we do offer

nodes.statement: Clinical negligence matters have access to the full set of MedBrief services, while personal injury matters have fewer services available.

nodes.label: Review-before-sorting is a preference flag, not a chargeable service

nodes.statement: The "review before sorting" wizard option is not a billable service but a preference flag that tells MedBrief the client wants to review their records before any further services proceed.

nodes.label: Early case assessment is performed on unsorted records for clinicians to assess whether there is a case

nodes.statement: The early case assessment service is performed on unsorted records: clinicians review them to determine whether a case exists.

nodes.label: MedBrief does early case assessments inside the platform

nodes.statement: A schedule of radiology is similar to a chronology but covers only radiology: a date-ordered list of all studies submitted, with their source.

nodes.label: Matter manager candidates are drawn from client-level admin users only

nodes.statement: The matter manager dropdown only shows users who have an administrative role (admin or super admin) added at the client level.

nodes.statement: Billing code is an optional field for most clients. For defendant firm Hampsons it is compulsory, and Hampsons users see a predefined drop-down of codes rather than a free-text field. Kennedy's is the other defendant firm but has not set up predefined codes.

nodes.statement: The team field is described as a client-internal use field, possibly used to indicate which internal team handles the matter (e.g. clinical negligence team vs personal injury), but Chantel is not certain of its exact use.

nodes.label: Additional contacts receive the same automated and digest emails as the matter manager

nodes.statement: Automated emails (records request receipts, clinical report progress, daily digest) go to both the matter manager and any additional contacts added to the matter, to accommodate PAs or secretaries.

nodes.label: Limitation date is the deadline by which a patient must make a claim

nodes.statement: The limitation date is the deadline by which the patient must bring a claim, analogous to a warranty period (three, five, or ten years depending on case type).

nodes.statement: Users can manually add an additional patient to any matter, but in practice this is almost always used for obstetrics mother-and-child cases.

nodes.statement: When an obstetrics case type (e.g. brain injury, maternal injury) is selected, the wizard automatically creates two patient records: one for the mother and one for the child.

provenance.quote: the claimant maybe you need to select is it the mom or is it the child it's usually the mom if the mom is not deceased

nodes.statement: For obstetrics matters the claimant selection defaults to the mother unless the mother is deceased, in which case the child may be the claimant.

nodes.label: The records request service page appears only when records request was selected as a service

nodes.statement: The records request wizard step is conditional: it is shown only when records request was selected as a service on the services step.

nodes.label: Trust list is predefined and pre-populated; users can add a new trust if not listed

nodes.statement: The trust / medical institution list on the records request step is pre-populated from a shared predefined list. If a trust is not in the list the user can add a new entry.

nodes.label: The matter name is auto-generated from the claimant name and the defendant (trust) name

nodes.statement: The matter name is automatically composed from the claimant's name and the defendant's name (in this demo, the trust).

nodes.statement: The sort-and-paginate wizard step allows users to choose between per-centre pagination (alphabetical or numerical labelling per centre) and continuous pagination, with a live preview panel on the right.

nodes.statement: The chronology page asks which patient(s) the chronology is for; the options depend on the case type — an obstetrics case offers mother, child, or both, while a single-patient case offers only one patient.

nodes.statement: Chantel is not certain whether a "don't know yet" option exists for the liability admitted field; she believes most cases are "no" and that "no" effectively covers the not-yet-decided state.

nodes.statement: The last step of the matter-creation wizard is a summary page listing all prior selections; the user can review, update, or change anything before finalising the matter.

nodes.label: The requires-pre-processing flag triggers AI unitisation then classification
nodes.statement: The 'requires pre-processing' checkbox on a sorting session triggers AI pre-processing: first unitisation (grouping pages for the same document in the correct order) and then classification (categorising each document by type, e.g. radiology, cardiology, clinical report, nurses notes).

nodes.label: Matter dashboard, the four views and the renewal cycle
nodes.summary: Chantel opens MedBrief logged in as a super-admin and walks the matter dashboard's four views (last viewed, added as fee earner, accepted invitations, renewals). She explains the two-year licence then annual renewal cycle and the 30/7/1-day reminder emails.

nodes.label: Creating a matter: client option and wizard versus classic modal
nodes.summary: She covers the three matter-creation entry points a super-admin sees (add client, classic, new wizard) and what clients see instead. The classic modal-and-flyout is contrasted with the newer matter-creation wizard, which is now the standard route.

nodes.label: Wizard overview document: default, service-dependent and summary pages
nodes.summary: Chantel shares a summary document that maps the wizard flow: the default pages everyone sees, the service-specific pages that appear based on first-page selections (chronology, records request), supporting documents for consent forms and death certificates, and the final summary page.

nodes.label: Live wizard services page and selecting clinical negligence services
nodes.summary: Working live in the Bulldog/Ditcher dev client, she explains client-level service defaults and the services page she dislikes. She picks clinical negligence over personal injury and steps through the service questions: records request, review before sorting, early case assessment, sorted records, chronology and schedule of radiology.

nodes.label: Matter detail page: manager, billing codes, contacts and case data
nodes.summary: She completes the matter detail pages: reference number, matter manager drawn from client admin users, optional billing code (compulsory and predefined for defendant firms like Hampsons), team and additional contacts who receive digest and automated emails. Then case type, allegations, claim value, dates of negligence and knowledge, and the limitation date.

nodes.label: Patient details and the mother-and-child obstetrics case
nodes.summary: Chantel fills in patient details (name, date of birth, deceased status) and shows how an obstetrics case type pre-populates two patients, mother and child. She covers adding additional patients and children, selecting the claimant, and naming defendants.

nodes.label: Records request service page and trust selection
nodes.summary: Because records request was selected, the wizard shows the records request page. She selects the trust or medical institution from a predefined list (with pre-populated fields), marks it as new records for both patients, and explains the against-trust checkbox and how the matter name is generated from claimant and defendant.

nodes.label: Sort and paginate settings and the chronology service page
nodes.summary: She walks the sort-and-paginate page (per-centre versus continuous pagination, prefixes, padding, page-number position and background) with its live preview. Then the chronology service page: chronology for which patient, key date range, and whether liability is admitted, prompting a discussion of the missing don't-know option.

nodes.label: Records destruction, supporting documents and creating the matter
nodes.summary: Chantel sets what happens to records once done (destroy-all by default), uploads the form-of-authority consent forms required per patient for a records request, notes the death certificate requirement for deceased patients, reviews the summary page and creates the matter.

nodes.label: Matter dashboard after creation: notes and records request widgets
nodes.summary: Landing on the new matter's dashboard, she shows the displayed patient details, the MedBrief-admin-only notes section with important-flag highlighting and sorting, and the two records request service widgets created for the two patients.

nodes.label: Confluence detour: matter flow diagram and the glossary
nodes.summary: She opens the MSR Products Confluence space, pointing to the matter flow diagram and the frequently-used-terms glossary. She defines the hierarchy of service group, service request and service option, and explains terms like fee earner.

nodes.label: Batches, clinical reports and additional requests on the dashboard
nodes.summary: Back on the dashboard she explains batches: linking or creating a batch request, that batches are only created when sorting or radiology is involved, and the hosting-only fallback. She covers the clinical reports group (early case assessment, chronology and schedule of radiology combined) and additional requests used for ad-hoc work and disclosures.

nodes.label: Creating a sorting session and AI pre-processing via DocSorter
nodes.summary: Chantel creates a sorting session, choosing between a client or MedBrief sort, selecting clinicians for each DocSorter stage (sorting, dating, admissions, file note and QA), and uploading a completed batch. She explains the requires-pre-processing flag that triggers AI unitisation and classification (the Sally system) before sending to the sorter.

nodes.label: Records page folder structure and the sorted-records flow
nodes.summary: She opens the records page with its folder tree and viewer, describing the four parent folders (records, case documentation, private folders, unsorted records), how private and case-documentation folders are auto-created per viewer or invited expert, and how sorted records are pushed from DocSorter into a ZZZ sorting-session folder for cleanup.

nodes.label: Grace Clayton example matter and the sorted index structure
nodes.summary: Using the Grace Clayton dev matter as a reference, Chantel shows the sorted index hierarchy: trust or institution, then admissions or events, then the records, repeated per patient so duplicates appear. She covers date ranges, page numbers and the bookmarks that mark updates added after a sorting session.

nodes.label: Expert folders, suppressed invitations and session wrap-up
nodes.summary: She explains case-documentation expert folders: the bell icon for disabled users whose folders are kept, and the suppressed-invitation workflow where a fee earner prepares an expert's folder before sending the invitation email from the users tab. The session closes with agreement to schedule a longer follow-up.
